teaching.title: C for Loop
teaching.analogy: C invented the modern for loop — three parts in one line: init, condition, update. Every language you'll ever use copied this design. It's 50 years old and still the most used loop structure.
teaching.content: for (init; condition; update) { body }. All three parts are optional. Init runs once. Condition tested before each iteration — 0 is false, non-zero is true. Update runs after each iteration.

examples.title: Classic C for Loop
examples.explanation: i declared before loop in older C (C89).

examples.title: Accumulator & Array Iteration

teaching.funFact: C89 didn't allow variable declarations inside for(int i=...) — you had to declare i before the loop. C99 relaxed this. Always compile C with -std=c99 or later to use modern features! 📅

teaching.title: Array Traversal in C
teaching.analogy: In C, arrays have no built-in length — you're the keeper of that information. Like a database with no record count field, you must always track the size manually.
teaching.content: C arrays don't store their length. You must track it manually via a variable or sizeof(). sizeof(arr)/sizeof(arr[0]) gives element count for stack arrays. Pointer arithmetic is C's powerful but risky alternative.

examples.title: Safe Array Traversal
examples.explanation: Track length with sizeof or a variable.

teaching.funFact: C's lack of array bounds checking is a massive source of security vulnerabilities — buffer overflows. The Heartbleed bug (2014) affected 500,000+ servers because of C array bounds violations! 🔐

mcq.explanation: i = 0,1,2,3,4 → 5 iterations. Standard pattern: 0 to <N gives N iterations.

mcq.q: How do you get the number of elements in a C array 'int arr[5]'?
mcq.explanation: sizeof: sizeof(arr) = total bytes. sizeof(arr[0]) = bytes per element. Division gives count. Only works on stack-allocated arrays (not pointers)!

mcq.q: In C99, can you declare 'int i' inside a for init?
mcq.explanation: C99: for(int i=0; i<n; i++) is valid in C99 and later. In C89, you must declare int i; before the loop. Always use -std=c99 or c11!

mcq.q: C for loop with all parts empty: for(;;) {} is:
mcq.explanation: Infinite Loop: for(;;) is C idiom for an intentional infinite loop — all three parts omitted. Equivalent to while(1). Use break to exit.

mcq.q: int s=0; for(int i=1;i<=4;i++) s+=i; s=?

coding.title: sizeof formula
coding.objective: C formula to get array element count from a stack array?
coding.starterCode:
int arr[5];
int len = sizeof(arr) / sizeof(arr[___]);
coding.hint: sizeof(arr[0]) gets size of first element — representative of all elements.

coding.title: Array last index
coding.objective: int a[]={1,2,3,4}. What is the last valid index?

options: i<=3 accesses arr[3] — out of bounds, undefined behavior! Use i<3
debugging.answer: i<=3 accesses arr[3] — out of bounds, undefined behavior! Use i<3
debugging.hint: arr[3] has indices 0,1,2. arr[3] is out of bounds — buffer overflow!

debugging.description:
🚨 Infinite Loop:

for (int i=10; i>0; i++) printf("%d\n", i);
options: i++ makes i grow — i>0 always true. Change to i-- to count down
debugging.answer: i++ makes i grow — i>0 always true. Change to i-- to count down
debugging.hint: Counting down requires i-- (decrement). i++ makes the condition stay true forever.

teaching.title: while Loop in C
teaching.analogy: C's while is the grandfather of all while loops. Check condition first, run body only if true. Identical syntax to Java — semicolons end statements, braces group blocks.
teaching.content: while (condition) { body } — condition tested BEFORE each iteration. Any non-zero value is true. Must update loop variable inside or risk infinite loop. while(1) is a common C idiom for infinite loops with break.

examples.title: C while Loop

teaching.funFact: while(1) is the idiomatic C infinite loop — more common than for(;;). You'll see it in embedded systems code that runs forever (firmware, OS kernels), breaking only on specific events! 🔄

teaching.title: do-while in C
teaching.analogy: do-while is C's 'at least once' guarantee. The body runs FIRST, then the condition is checked. The semicolon after the while clause is easy to forget!
teaching.content: do { body } while (condition); — note the semicolon. Body runs at least once. Common in C for reading user input in terminal programs and state machines.

examples.title: C do-while

teaching.funFact: do-while is used extensively in C macro definitions. The pattern 'do { code } while(0)' is a famous C trick for multi-statement macros that safely work in all contexts! 🔧

options: An intentional infinite loop (use break to exit)
mcq.explanation: while(1): 1 is non-zero = always true. Classic C idiom for infinite loops — used in embedded systems, OS kernels, and server loops. Use break to exit.

mcq.q: C do-while requires what after the closing )?
mcq.explanation: Semicolon: do { } while(condition); needs ; after ). Missing it is a common compile error.

mcq.q: int x=10; while(x<5){x++;} How many times runs?
mcq.explanation: Never: x=10, x<5 is 0 (false) from the start. while checks FIRST.

mcq.q: do{ printf('A'); }while(0); How many times prints?
mcq.explanation: Once: do-while always runs ONCE regardless of condition. Then checks while(0) — 0 is false, stops. Famous 'do-once' pattern!

coding.title: while true idiom
coding.objective: C idiom for intentional infinite loop?
coding.hint: 1 is always non-zero = always true in C.

coding.title: do-while runs count
coding.hint: do-while always runs at least once. Then checks while(0) — false, stops.

options: Missing semicolon — do-while must end with ; → } while(count>0);
debugging.answer: Missing semicolon — do-while must end with ; → } while(count>0);
debugging.hint: do { } while(condition); — the semicolon after ) is mandatory!

teaching.title: break, continue & goto in C
teaching.analogy: break is an ejector seat — instant exit. continue is 'skip this round.' C also has goto — a teleporter. goto gets a bad reputation but is occasionally used in C for cleanup on error paths in kernel code.
teaching.content: break: exits innermost loop or switch. continue: skips to next iteration. C also has goto which jumps to a label — controversial but used in C kernel code for centralized error cleanup.

examples.title: break and continue in C
goto (C only) — error cleanup
examples.explanation: goto used legitimately for cleanup in C.

teaching.funFact: Linus Torvalds (Linux creator) explicitly allows goto in the Linux kernel source code — arguing it produces cleaner error-handling code than deeply nested if/else chains. goto is NOT evil in C! 🐧

mcq.q: What does break do in a C loop?
mcq.explanation: break: Exits the innermost loop or switch statement immediately. Execution resumes after the closing }.

mcq.q: continue in a C for loop causes:
options: Skip remainder of body, run update, re-check condition
mcq.explanation: continue: Skips rest of current iteration. In for: runs update (i++) then checks condition. Loop continues with next iteration.

mcq.q: C has a jump statement that Java does NOT have. What is it?
mcq.explanation: goto: C has goto which jumps to a labeled line. Java removed goto (it's a reserved word but unused). goto is controversial but found in Linux kernel for error cleanup.
continue: When i=3, skip printf. Output: 0 1 2 4.

coding.title: break exit value
coding.hint: At i=6 → break. Last print was i=5.

options: break after first printf — only prints 0 and exits. break should be inside an if condition
debugging.answer: break after first printf — only prints 0 and exits. break should be inside an if condition
debugging.hint: Unconditional break always exits after first iteration. Use if(i==X) break.

teaching.title: ⚠️ BOSS — THE C RUNTIME
teaching.analogy: The C Runtime manages every loop in your program — stack frames, loop counters, bounds checks (that you must do yourself!). Defeat it by proving your loops are tight, correct, and never overflow.
teaching.content: Tests ALL Level 3 C: for with C99 init, sizeof for array length, while/do-while, while(1) idiom, break/continue/goto, nested loops, buffer overflows from bad loop bounds.

examples.title: Full C Loop Arsenal
examples.code:
✅ for(int i=0;i<n;i++) — C99 style
✅ sizeof(arr)/sizeof(arr[0]) — array length
✅ while(condition) — check first
✅ while(1) + break — infinite loop idiom
✅ do{ }while(cond); — semicolon!
✅ break/continue/goto
✅ i<len NOT i<=len — avoid overflow

teaching.funFact: The C runtime has no safety net. Every undefined behavior (wrong loop bounds, reading past array end) can corrupt memory silently, crash later, or create security holes. The compiler won't always catch it! 🔴

mcq.q: What does for(;;) do in C?
mcq.explanation: Infinite Loop: for(;;) has no condition — always true. Equivalent to while(1). Classic C idiom for server loops and firmware.
sizeof(a)=16 bytes (4 ints × 4 bytes each). sizeof(a[0])=4. 16/4=4 elements.
Once: do-while always runs once. Then checks while(0) — false. Prints A once.
Even i skipped by continue. Prints only odd: 1, 3.

mcq.q: Which statement is ONLY in C and REMOVED from Java?
mcq.explanation: goto: C has goto; Java reserved the keyword but never implemented it. C uses goto for legitimate error-cleanup patterns in kernel/embedded code.

coding.title: Boss: Array safe bound
coding.objective: int arr[5]. Correct loop condition to avoid overflow?
coding.starterCode:
int arr[5];
for(int i=0; i ___ 5; i++) printf("%d\n",arr[i]);
coding.hint: i < 5 gives indices 0-4. i <= 5 accesses arr[5] — out of bounds!

options: i<=3 reads arr[3] which is out of bounds — undefined behavior. Use i<3
debugging.answer: i<=3 reads arr[3] which is out of bounds — undefined behavior. Use i<3
debugging.hint: 3 elements → indices 0,1,2. i<=3 tries arr[3] → buffer overflow!
